title: The Ecosystem of GPUs for Gen AI Engineers — Part 3/3 | by Vinodh Thiagarajan | Medium
text:
The Ecosystem of GPUs for Gen AI Engineers — Part 3/3
In Part 1, we built a vocabulary: TFLOPS, HBM, precision formats, memory bandwidth. In Part 2, we compared hardware: the A100, H100, and B200. Now comes the question that matters most in practice: how do you actually get access to these GPUs?
The answer isn’t purely technical. It’s about supply chains, geopolitics, interconnects, and cloud economics. You can’t run GPUs you can’t get. And getting them — at scale, reliably, affordably — requires understanding the ecosystem around them.
So let’s start at the beginning: where these chips actually come from.
The Company Behind Every GPU: TSMC
Here’s something that might surprise you: NVIDIA doesn’t manufacture its own GPUs its Fabless. Neither does AMD. Or Apple. Or Qualcomm.Tesla too I guess.
They all rely on one company: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC).
Every A100, H100, and B200 we discussed in Part 2? Made by TSMC. The chip in your iPhone? TSMC. The processor in your MacBook? TSMC. This single company manufactures roughly 90% of the world’s most advanced chips.
TSMC’s top customers read like a who’s-who of tech: Apple (~25% of revenue), NVIDIA (~11–20%), AMD, Qualcomm, Broadcom, MediaTek, and even Intel outsources some chips to them. When NVIDIA announced record demand for H100s, TSMC’s advanced packaging capacity became the bottleneck.
The Geopolitical Elephant
Here’s where it gets uncomfortable: Taiwan sits 100 miles from mainland China, across the Taiwan Strait. China claims Taiwan as part of its territory. The world’s most advanced chip manufacturing — the chips powering AI, smartphones, military systems, and modern infrastructure — is concentrated on an island at the center of one of the world’s most volatile geopolitical tensions.
This concentration creates what’s been called Taiwan’s “silicon shield” — the idea that Taiwan’s semiconductor dominance makes it too economically critical to attack. Disrupting TSMC would cost the global economy an estimated $2.7 trillion (for a blockade) to $10 trillion (for a full conflict), according to various estimates.
⚠️ The Dependency Problem: The U.S. buys ~92% of its advanced AI chips from Taiwan. If something disrupts TSMC’s Taiwan operations — whether conflict, natural disaster, or political tension — the entire AI industry grinds to a halt. There is no backup.
The Arizona Gambit
Recognizing this vulnerability, the U.S. government passed the CHIPS and Science Act to incentivize domestic chip manufacturing. TSMC has committed $165 billion to build fabs in Arizona — making it one of the largest foreign investments in U.S. history.
But there’s a catch (there’s always a catch). The first Arizona fab is operational, but the next two won’t be ready until 2028 and 2030 — reshoring takes years. Manufacturing in Arizona costs significantly more than in Taiwan, with rumors suggesting 30% higher wafer prices. TSMC’s Taiwan operations benefit from a “one-hour ecosystem” where everything they need is within an hour’s reach; Arizona doesn’t have that yet. And even with the Arizona expansion, only ~30% of TSMC’s advanced capacity will be in the U.S. Taiwan remains dominant.
TSMC’s founder, Morris Chang, has been blunt about the challenges, calling the U.S. expansion “a very expensive exercise” and noting that American manufacturing costs are 50% higher than Taiwan.
Why This Matters for Gen AI Engineers
You might be thinking: “I just want to train models. Why do I care about Taiwanese geopolitics?”
Because it affects you directly. When TSMC can’t produce enough advanced packaging (CoWoS), H100 availability drops and prices spike. Any geopolitical escalation could disrupt GPU supply chains for months or years. Providers with pre-secured GPU allocations become more valuable during shortages. And as U.S. manufacturing scales up, expect some GPU prices to reflect higher production costs.
The AI boom is, in many ways, a TSMC boom. Understanding this dependency helps you plan more realistically.
NVLink: How GPUs Talk to Each Other
Once you have GPUs, the next question is: how do they communicate? This matters more than you might think.
The standard way to connect GPUs to a system is PCIe (Peripheral Component Interconnect Express) — the same interface used by SSDs, network cards, and other expansion cards. PCIe Gen5, the current standard, delivers about 128 GB/s of bidirectional bandwidth per x16 slot.
That sounds fast. But consider: an H100 has 3.35 TB/s of memory bandwidth internally. If two H100s need to share data over PCIe, they’re limited to 128 GB/s — less than 4% of their internal bandwidth. For workloads that require frequent GPU-to-GPU communication (like training large models across multiple GPUs), PCIe becomes a severe bottleneck.
This is why NVIDIA created NVLink.
NVLink is NVIDIA’s proprietary point-to-point interconnect that bypasses PCIe entirely. Instead of going through the CPU and system bus, GPUs connect directly to each other. The numbers tell the story: NVLink 4 on the H100 delivers 900 GB/s of bidirectional bandwidth — 7× faster than PCIe Gen5. NVLink 5 on the B200 pushes this to 1.8 TB/s.
When Do You Actually Need NVLink?
Not every workload benefits from NVLink. Here’s the practical breakdown:
You need NVLink when: training large models that don’t fit in a single GPU’s memory (tensor parallelism requires constant communication between GPUs), running inference on models that span multiple GPUs, or doing any workload where GPUs need to share intermediate results frequently.
You might not need NVLink when: running multiple independent inference jobs (each fits in one GPU), doing data-parallel training where GPUs only sync gradients periodically, or working with smaller models that fit comfortably in a single GPU.
The rule of thumb: if your model fits in one GPU’s memory and you’re just adding more GPUs for throughput (not for model size), PCIe might be fine. But if GPUs need to act as one unified compute resource — sharing memory, passing tensors back and forth — NVLink becomes essential.
Multi-GPU Configurations: DGX, NVSwitch, and Beyond
So how do these NVLink connections actually get wired up? You can’t just plug NVLink cables between any two GPUs. The topology matters.
NVIDIA’s solution is the NVSwitch — a dedicated chip that acts as a high-bandwidth fabric connecting multiple GPUs. Think of it as a network switch, but for GPUs, running at NVLink speeds.
The DGX H100 is NVIDIA’s flagship AI server. It packs 8 H100 GPUs connected by 4 NVSwitch chips, creating a fully-connected mesh where every GPU can communicate with every other GPU at full NVLink 4 speed (900 GB/s). The result: 640 GB of unified, cache-coherent memory accessible by any GPU without going through the CPU.
For even larger scale, NVIDIA offers NVLink Switch — external switches that extend NVLink across multiple servers. A “SuperPOD” configuration can connect up to 256 H100s (32 DGX nodes) with 57.6 TB/s of all-to-all bandwidth. The Blackwell generation pushes this further: the GB200 NVL72 connects 72 Blackwell GPUs into a single domain with 130 TB/s of aggregate bandwidth.
SXM vs PCIe: The Form Factor Question
You’ll notice GPUs come in two form factors: SXM and PCIe. We touched on this in Part 2, but it’s worth revisiting in the context of multi-GPU systems.
SXM (Server PCI Express Module) is NVIDIA’s proprietary mezzanine format designed specifically for high-density servers. SXM GPUs plug directly into a baseboard, enabling NVLink connections and higher power delivery. PCIe GPUs use the standard expansion card format — they’re more flexible (can go in any PCIe slot) but limited to PCIe bandwidth for GPU-to-GPU communication.
The practical upshot: if you need multi-GPU training with high-bandwidth interconnects, you’re looking at SXM-based systems (DGX, HGX, or cloud instances built on them). If you’re doing inference or workloads that don’t require tight GPU coupling, PCIe systems may be more cost-effective.
Cloud Providers: Where to Actually Get GPUs
Unless you’re running your own data center (and can afford the ~$300–500K for a single DGX H100), you’re renting GPUs from cloud providers. The landscape here has changed dramatically over the past two years.
The market splits into two categories: hyperscalers (AWS, Google Cloud, Azure) and GPU-specialized providers (Lambda Labs, RunPod, Vast.ai etc).
The Hyperscaler Trade-off
AWS, Google, and Azure offer reliability, global availability, and deep integration with their broader ecosystems. Need to plug your training job into SageMaker? Connect to BigQuery? Use Azure’s compliance certifications for healthcare data? The hyperscalers make that easy.
But you pay for it. Hyperscaler pricing runs 2–3× higher than specialized providers for the same GPU. And there are hidden costs: data egress fees ($0.08-$0.12 per GB), storage costs, cross-region transfer fees. A 10TB dataset on fast storage can add $1,000-$3,000/month to your bill.
The Specialized Provider Advantage
Companies like Lambda Labs and RunPod focus specifically on GPU compute for AI workloads. They’re typically 40–60% cheaper than hyperscalers, with simpler pricing (many include free egress) and environments pre-configured for ML work.
The trade-offs: smaller ecosystems, fewer regions, and sometimes capacity constraints. During the H100 shortage of 2023–2024, availability was a real issue — you’d sign up and wait weeks for capacity. That’s eased somewhat, but popular GPUs can still be constrained.
Spot Instances: The Budget Option
All major providers offer “spot” or “preemptible” instances at 40–90% discounts. The catch: your instance can be terminated with as little as 30 seconds notice when capacity is needed elsewhere.
For training workloads with proper checkpointing, spot instances are excellent — you checkpoint frequently, and if you get preempted, you resume from the last checkpoint. For production inference that can’t tolerate interruption, stick to on-demand.
Emerging Alternatives
The landscape keeps evolving. Decentralized GPU networks (Vast.ai’s marketplace model) aggregate capacity from distributed providers, sometimes offering the lowest prices. Some startups are building platforms specifically for inference (lower latency, optimized pricing). And hardware diversity is increasing — AMD’s MI300X and Intel’s Gaudi chips offer alternatives to NVIDIA, though the software ecosystem remains NVIDIA-dominant.
The Ecosystem Is the Bottleneck
Here’s the uncomfortable truth: the limiting factor for most AI projects isn’t the model architecture. It’s the infrastructure.
You can design the most elegant training pipeline in the world, but if TSMC can’t produce enough advanced packaging, you won’t get GPUs. If you don’t understand NVLink topology, you’ll waste money on hardware configurations that don’t help your workload. If you pick the wrong cloud provider, you’ll either overpay or find yourself stuck in a queue waiting for capacity.
This three-part series was an attempt to build a mental model for all of this. Part 1 gave you the vocabulary to read spec sheets critically. Part 2 showed you how the generations of hardware compare. And Part 3 — this one — tried to map the ecosystem that determines whether you can actually get your hands on that hardware.
I wrote this series because I needed it myself. Coming from a data engineering background, I kept hitting walls trying to understand the Gen AI infrastructure landscape. The information was scattered across vendor whitepapers, blog posts, and institutional knowledge. I wanted something that connected the dots.
There’s plenty I didn’t cover: training optimization techniques, quantization strategies, the software stack (CUDA, cuDNN, NCCL), inference serving architectures. Each of those could be its own series. For now, I hope this gives one a foundation to build on.
I’m learning alongside you.
Vinodh
Stay Hungry! Stay Foolish!
